i tls i',, '91
GEORCE BASYE
October 25, L991
Fair Political Practi-ce Commissi-on
428 J Street
Sacramento, California 95814
Re: Reclamation District No. 1oo4
Gentlemen:
Reclamation District No. 1oo4 reguests advice regarding
the ability of the members of its Board of Trustees to
act upon an allocation of funds to its landowners
arising frorn participation of the District in the State
Emergency Drought Water Bank during the L99L
irrigation 
season.
Approximately 7,Ooo acres were irrigated within the
Reclamation District during L991. Of these acres,
41000 acres were irrigated from the Districtrs water
supply, and 3,000 acres $/ere irrigated through
activation by landowners of their own ground water
welIs as an alternate supply. This activation of
ground water wells, which would otherwise not have been
utilized during the L99L season, allowed the District
to reduce its use of water from the Sacramento River
and be compensated for that reduction in use through a
contract with the State Department of Water Resources
State EmergTency Water Bank Program. A question has
arisen as to the distribution of the funds attributable
to that reduction in use by the District for which the
District was corf,pensated by the Department of Water
Resources.
The District was not sufficiently conservative (as it
turns out) in its estimate of the water supply which
would be availabl-e to the District after deletion from
the projected service area of the acreage to be
irrigated from ground water wells. 
The District
estimated that it would have an adequate supply left
from the river to serve the balance of the acreage not
participating in the Water Bank. As it turned out,
that assumption was not correct, as the water supply
was less than projected and, accordinglY, a deficiency
resulted in the water supply to the District.


DOWNEY
BRAND
SEYMOUR
& ROHWER
Fair Political Practice Commission
october 25, L99t
Page 2
The question before the Board of Trustees is whether
that deficiency in the water supply and resultant loss
in value of the lrater transferred should be al,Iocated
(1) totally to the well water users who assumed they
woul-d receive the benefit of all the paynent for the
water Bank participation funnelled through the
District, 
or, (2) a portj,on of that deficiency should
be applied to all users in the Districtrs 
systern
including those who did not participate in the water
Bank Program.
The inpact of the shortage of water is equivalent to
approxinately $400,o00 in value which nust be allocated
entirely against the well $/ater users as a reduction
from the anount of reimbursement to them from the water
Bank funds, or allocated in whole or in part to those
lands which vere not irrigated from we11s and $/ere
dependent upon the District's 
system during the 1991
season.
The District has a Board of Trustees composed of five
mernbers. Three of those Trustees did not participate
in the water Bank operation and $/ouLd be benefitted if
the charge were allocated entirely to the well $/ater
users. Tsro of those Trustees participated in the water
Bank operation and would be adversely impacted if the
deficiency were charged entirely to the well water
users, and benefitted if the deficiency were charged
agaj-nst all of the lands in the District, or against
those not participating in the water Bank. rt is
anticipated that either decision might be challenged on
the basis of the potential conflict of the Trustees
aaking up the najorir-ir of the decision in either
direction. 
Accordingty, the Trustees have requested
that we, as their attorneys, submit this issue to the
FPPC for advice as to hor., the Board of Trustees might
properly approve a decision as to the allocation of
these funds.
one member of the Board of Trustees represents an area
of the District which would not be inpacted either
favorably or unfavorably by either decision' That
Trustee iould appear to be essentially neutral on this
issue to the exlent of any financiat irnpact upon the
portion of the District represented by that Trustee, or
upon his own private financial interest.


DOWNEY
BRAND
SEYMOUR
& ROHWER
Fair Political Practice Commission
October 25, L99L
Page 3
Your advice as to how the District may compose a
majority which would decide this issue is requested.
one suggestion which has been made is that an
arbitrator be selected to reach this decision for the
Board so that it would be taken completely out of the
hands of the Trustees. We would appreciate your advice
as to whether that is an appropriate solution.
u;.&
GEORGE BASYE
Attorney for Reclamation
District No. L0o4
cc: Reclamation District No. l-004
GB: jplEPPC

sections.question: Whether members of the Board of Trustees of Reclamation District No. 1004 can properly act upon an allocation of funds to landowners arising from participation in the State Emergency Drought Water Bank, given that some Trustees have financial interests in the outcome depending on how a water supply deficiency is allocated among different groups of landowners. Specifically, whether the Board can approve a decision on allocating approximately $400,000 in value attributable to a water supply shortfall, and whether using an arbitrator to make the decision instead of the Trustees would be an appropriate solution.
sections.question_synthetic: Can the Board of Trustees of Reclamation District No. 1004 properly vote on allocating a water supply deficiency among landowners when some Trustees have conflicting financial interests depending on the allocation decision, and is using an arbitrator an appropriate alternative?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response. Document is from a law firm (Downey Brand Seymour & Rohwer) requesting FPPC advice on behalf of a reclamation district. No FPPC conclusion/response is included in this document. The question involves potential conflicts of interest under FPPC jurisdiction. Confidence slightly reduced due to no clear section headers and complex fact pattern requiring interpretation.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether members of the Board of Trustees of Reclamation District No. 1004 can properly act upon an allocation of funds to landowners arising from participation in the State Emergency Drought Water Bank, given that some Trustees have financial interests in the outcome depending on how a water supply deficiency is allocated among different groups of landowners. Specifically, whether the Board can approve a decision on allocating approximately $400,000 in value attributable to a water supply shortfall, and whether using an arbitrator to make the decision instead of the Trustees would be an appropriate solution.

embedding.summary: This is a request for FPPC advice from attorneys representing Reclamation District No. 1004 regarding whether Board Trustees with potential conflicts of interest can vote on how to allocate a $400,000 water supply deficiency among district landowners after a state emergency drought water bank program.
